topic: Cursor Rules Guide 2026: How to Write .cursorrules for Your Project

competitors.title: .cursorrules Guide: How to Write Effective Cursor Rules (2026)
key_points: Explains .cursorrules file format with recommended structure for project context, tech stack, code style, and component guidelines
Covers four rule activation modes: Always Apply, Manual, Model Decision, and Glob (file pattern matching)
Provides complete .cursorrules examples for React/Next.js and Python/FastAPI projects
Compares .cursorrules vs other AI config files (CLAUDE.md, copilot-instructions.md, .windsurfrules)
Best practices: keep rules concise (under 500 lines), use direct tone, include real examples, address edge cases

competitors.title: Cursor Rules: Complete .mdc Guide & 15 Templates (2026)
key_points: Covers the transition from legacy .cursorrules single file to modern .cursor/rules/ directory with .mdc files
Explains four rule activation modes: Always Apply, Auto Attached (globs), Agent Requested (description-based), and Manual (@mention)
Key insight: keep Always Apply rules under 200 words to avoid the 'token tax' — every token loads in every request
15 ready-to-use templates covering React components, API routes, Supabase, Stripe payments, Prisma, shadcn/ui, testing, security, and performance
Anatomy of .mdc rule: YAML frontmatter (description, globs, alwaysApply) + markdown body with instructions
Commit .cursor/rules/ to version control so entire team shares same AI behavior and coding standards

competitors.title: Cursor Rules Examples and Templates: A Practical Guide for 2026
key_points: Covers both .cursorrules (legacy) and .cursor/rules/*.mdc (recommended) formats with practical migration guidance
Seven copy-pasteable templates for React/Next.js, Python/FastAPI, and other common stacks
Four rule types table: Always (every chat), Auto (glob-based file matching), Agent Requested (AI decides), Manual (explicit reference)
Practical advice: start small, add rules only when the agent repeats the same mistake twice
Emphasizes rule organization for large projects with multiple distinct layers (frontend/backend/tests)

competitors.title: Cursor Rules Best Practices: Complete .mdc Guide (2026)
key_points: Declares .cursorrules deprecated in favor of .mdc files in .cursor/rules/ with frontmatter for scope control
Best practice #1: Start small, write rules only when AI repeats mistakes — don't write 20 rules on day one
Best practice #2: Keep rules under 500 lines — each rule consumes context window tokens
Best practice #3: Reference files, don't copy them — 'Follow the pattern in src/components/Button.tsx' stays current
Best practice #4: Specify SDK versions explicitly — AI models mix syntax across library versions
Best practice #5: Use strong language — 'NEVER use class components' works better than 'Prefer functional components'
Best practice #6: Include verification steps so the agent can self-check its work
Context budget analysis: 1,000-line rule file eats 2,000+ tokens before model reads your prompt

stats.fact: Cursor surpassed 1 million total users by late 2025, with 360,000+ paying subscribers and 36% free-to-paid conversion rate
Anysphere (Cursor's parent company) reached $29.3 billion valuation in November 2025 after $2.3B Series D funding
Cursor's ARR surpassed $1 billion in 2025, growing from $1M to $100M in ~12 months — one of the fastest SaaS growth trajectories in history
84% of developers now use or plan to use AI coding tools in their workflow (2025 Stack Overflow Developer Survey), up from 76% the year before
stats.source: Stack Overflow Developer Survey 2025 via vibecodingacademy.ai

stats.fact: Cursor is used by tens of thousands of teams at companies including Nvidia, Adobe, Uber, Shopify, PayPal, Stripe, Coinbase, OpenAI, and Midjourney
Cursor has 300+ employees and launched Cursor 2.0 on October 29, 2025
Modern .cursor/rules .mdc files were introduced as the recommended format, replacing the legacy single .cursorrules file for better scope control and token efficiency
Always Apply rules should stay under 200 words to minimize 'token tax' — every token loads in every single AI request
